topic: Why Bread Goes Stale Faster in the Fridge

packaging.title: Why the Fridge Makes Bread Stale Faster
altTitles: The Real Reason Bread Goes Stale in the Fridge
Your Fridge Is Staling Your Bread on Purpose
packaging.description: A look at the food science of starch retrogradation and why refrigerating bread speeds up staling instead of preserving it, plus the temperature that actually keeps bread fresh.

thumbnail.visualIdea: A single bread slice halfway inside an open fridge, its cut edge visibly dried and cracking with crumb dust falling

packaging.hookPromise: You put bread in the fridge to keep it fresh, but you're actually making it go stale up to six times faster than leaving it on the counter — and here's the molecule responsible.
openLoops: If cold ruins bread, why does the freezer somehow save it?
Why does microwaving stale bread make it soft again for a few minutes?
What's the one temperature zone that stales bread the fastest, and your fridge sits right in it?
packaging.payoff: Staling isn't bread drying out — it's starch molecules recrystallizing (retrogradation), and that recrystallization happens fastest at fridge temperatures around 4°C, while freezing stops it cold and reheating temporarily melts it back to soft.
packaging.whyItWorks: Nearly everyone has refrigerated bread assuming it helps, so the counterintuitive 'you're doing it backwards' gap has huge broad appeal and a clean molecular payoff that fits the niche.
